description: Thursday 18 June 2026 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Defence; 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Defence Force Hearing of evidence: Submitter list: Hon Chris Penk, Minister of Defence and Minister for Veterans Ministry of Defence Brook Barrington, Secretary of Defence Sarah Minson, Deputy Secretary, Capability Delivery Ant Shaw, Acting Chief Financial Officer New Zealand Defence Force Air Marshal Tony Davies, Chief Of Defence Force Rear Admiral Mat Williams, Vice Chief of Defence Force Mr Alex Brunt, Head of Veterans Affairs Ms Jacinda Funnell, Chief People Officer Ms Bridget Marks, Chief Financial Officer Brigadier James Kennedy-Good, Director Defence Legal Brigadier Matt Weston, Assistant Chief Capability Mr Mark Brunton, Head Defence Estate and Infrastructure
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Defence; 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Defence Force. Hon Chris Penk, Minister of Defence and Minister for Veterans. Ministry of Defence

chunks.text: Oh, we're back. Yes, sir. Jumping up and down. Sorry.
Good morning, thank you all. All right, thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

Very good. All right. Good morning, Minister. Welcome along to the estimates hearing for Vote Defense and Vote Defense Force. Your first time in the main chair for this, appreciate that, and congratulations again on that opportunity. Welcome also to Chief of Defense Force, Air Marshal Tony Davies, and Secretary of Defense, Brooke Berrington, and your respective teams.

We have two hours set down for the hearing today. We welcome comments from you. You'll have seen the proposed agenda, giving a broad outline of the sections we'd like to cover. We'll work our way through that, but of course, maintaining flexibility to focus on areas of priority for members of the committee as we go. But I'd like to hand over to you for some introductory comments.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. Morena, good morning to all the members of the committee. I'm really delighted to have this opportunity to tell the stories of the wonderful work that Defense does for New Zealand. Of course, I'm delighted to be here with the Chief of Defense Force and the Secretary of Defense, as you've rightly acknowledged. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And of course, right behind us, so to speak, but also quite literally, I'll have them to excuse my back, many good men and women of the New Zealand Defense Force, the Ministry of

Defense. And I'd be remiss if I didn't start by acknowledging the work that our uniformed personnel, along with Defense civilians, and of course, MOD team, do in defense of our nation. I'm grateful personally to them, of course, on behalf of government, and I know that across the Parliament there's a strong and rightful recognition of that. And can I say, I've always had really good interactions with this committee. I do want to acknowledge, if you'll indulge me for a moment, relatively new in his counterpart role, Honorable Damien O'Connor.

And for the last time that we'll do this together, Mr. Gregor O'Connor. So thank you for the opportunity to interact with the committee on these important matters. I'll keep my opening remarks really brief, because obviously I welcome the opportunity for the questions and comments that the committee may have. But through you, Mr. Chair, I'd also respectfully suggest that if we were to give an equivalent opportunity to both of these gentlemen, that would be helpful to set the scene. In terms of the recent budget, which is, of course,

nominally the focus of today's session, I was pleased that Budget 2026 reflected the government's commitment to support Defense to do the important work that it does. Of course, there was the headline figure of the $1.58 billion expenditure, broken down to $700 and $880 million worth of capital and operating expenditure. And of course, within that, a number of key aims need to be met.

Some of those revolve around new platforms for the particular effects, and we can obviously talk about that in as much detail as you wish. But of course, also investing in our people is crucially important. So I was pleased that we have been able to state clearly that we will focus on ensuring that we retain, particularly retain, yes, continue to recruit, but also retain the sailors, soldiers, and aviators that we need to continue to deliver the important effects through our Defense Force. And included in that is, of course,

continuing to improve the defense estate and infrastructure, particularly where accommodation is concerned. That is an aspect of the preparedness of our people to serve, so it's important that we continue to invest strongly in that regard. I will say that for me personally, that's something of a passion project. I was very pleased to have been given the delegation, among a couple of others, for defense estate and infrastructure in my previous role as Associate Minister of Defense, of course, under our colleague and friend, former colleague now, the Honorable

Judith Collins, of course, in her new role as Minister of Defense. So I'm pleased to be able to do my honest attempt to continue to carry on that good work. So with that, Mr. Chair, through you, I will say again that I look forward to interacting with yourselves. And I look actually across the committee, and I see no fewer than three fellow parliamentarians who have worn the uniform in one color or another, including a couple of ex-Army Territorial Force officers and, of course, ex-Wing Commander Crossley.

So to you and of course, all members of the committee, look forward to our discussion today. Thank you. Did you wish to have opening remarks from the Secretary of Defense? Yeah, if it's okay with you, I'll pass to Chief of Defense Force and or the Minister, excuse me, Secretary of Defense. Okay, shall I go? You go. Good morning, Mr. Chair and members. Thank you for this opportunity to address the committee.

Ministry of Defense has been tasked with preparing and equipping a defense force that can respond to the various challenges that New Zealand may face. Whether that's supporting communities impacted by severe weather events or deploying in support of our collective security interests, it's a responsibility we take seriously.

That's why we are focused on three things, three main things. Delivering the 2025 Defense Capability Plan Working with our partners to ensure that our bonds, which are based on trust, are strong and resilient, and working with industry to deliver capability faster, but without losing rigor.

We are focused on delivering existing defense projects to time, while providing value for money and innovative thinking. And we are also progressing the first of the two-year reviews of the Defense Capability Plan in 2027. It's what we're focused on at the moment. I now hand over to the Chief of the Defense Force.

Thank you, Secretary, and thank you to the Chair and to committee for the opportunity to be here today and to make some opening remarks. The New Zealand Defense Force is responding to a more volatile, a more contested, and a less predictable world by sharpening our focus on readiness, our operational delivery, and working very closely with our partners.

Keeping our core purpose firmly in focus is critical in order for us to provide the right options to government, credible combat capable options. That means behind the scenes, lifting readiness across the force, embedding a stronger war fighting ethos, and preparing for more demanding operations alongside our partners if needed.

At the same time, the New Zealand Defense Force continues to deliver at pace. We are active across domestic, regional, and global operations. We're protecting our maritime approaches. We're contributing to longstanding, ongoing international missions. And we are responding whenever New Zealanders need us most.

These tasks increasingly overlap, and they require a defense force that is flexible, responsive, and able to shift quickly between roles and tasks. We are rebuilding and strengthening the foundations of the Defense Force. Our people are central to everything we do, and effort is going into stabilizing the workforce, generating critical skills, and ensuring our personnel and their families are properly supported, because readiness ultimately depends on them.

Alongside this, we are bringing in new capabilities and making the most of recent investments. This includes new technologies, strengthening maritime capability in particular, and increasing our interoperability with partners through both training and operations.

All of this is being delivered with a clear focus on urgency, but also value for money, and ensuring effort and resources are not wasted and directed where they have the greatest impact on operational readiness. In short, the NZDF is actively adapting to meet the demands set by government, delivering today while building the capability and readiness required for the challenges ahead. Thank you.

Very good. All right. Thank you very much. We'll jump straight into it. So now with the overview of appropriations, first question from the Honorable David. Look, thank you very much and welcome to the committee, and congratulations on getting more money, I guess, for defense in a very tight budget. But as was said, one of the big challenges is retention.

That is the personnel, which is the heart and soul of course, of any defense force. And when I look down at the additional funding that has gone in, $43.5 million for cost increases just to develop a plan, and only $30 million for remuneration.

So my question to you is, have we still got our priorities wrong in actually looking after the people on the ground, their salaries, their conditions, and in fact, their housing, which is acknowledged, the basic conditions, is still something that we are undercooking, or you are in the budget in our approach?

Yeah. Thank you very much for the question. I think it's a very reasonable and appropriate line of questioning, that we must ensure that our personnel are sufficiently well looked after to wish to continue to serve. Of course, that's not just a matter of doing the right thing by our people, but of course, the personnel whom we rely upon to operate in the different domains will enable us to be operationally effective.

I would say that across the four-year envelope of funding identified in the budget, that with $174 million to meet the cost increase to ensure that the personnel and equipment are ready to deploy, along with 120 million to bring Defense Force pay closer to market rates, and I emphasize closer, point to which I'll return briefly finally.

And also to meet non-discretionary remuneration increases, that which is effectively automatic or determined externally. I think it's fair to say that we have made a visible commitment in this space. Of course, it's always possible to say that more can and should be done. But for now, I'm pleased that we are making steps in the right direction.

And more particularly on the question of pay rates being brought closer to market rates, it seems to me that for reasons of funding levels over many years, successive governments, that we have some catching up to do. I'm pleased that we are making steps in the right direction.

But as with the Defense Capability Plan itself, broadly across all the different categories of investment that we need, we identify that ongoing work is needed. And indeed, that has been the commitment of the government through Defense Capability Plan 2025, to continue to increase as necessary to ensure that those defense aims are met As I say, with people being an important part, and as you've quite rightly acknowledged along the same lines.

Can I then just step back from that, highlighting the importance, I guess, of people all the way through. Defense Capability Plan, very bold, ambitious statements made at the launch of that, and I know now the minister's moved on to greener pastures and left the mantle with you. 2% of GDP is an ambitious task, and it's a lot of money.

How well are you going in maintaining that trajectory? Yeah . Thanks for the question. Of course, it's important that we do as we say, and following those important statements made by my predecessor on behalf of government with the release of Defense Capability Plan 2025, hereafter DCP.

I think it's fair to say that we know that we need to ensure that our actions match our words. So far, that has been the case, with around the 1.2% of GDP mark now representing the trajectory, very much tracking the line that you would expect between two points of, roughly speaking, 1% of GDP, to the 2% mark in the year 2032, 2033. So we are on the right path, but we do need to stay that course, and we're conscious of that.

Supplementary from- Yes... the supplementary on that. Just acknowledging the comments my colleague's made around the defense estate. I think it's quite timely as well, and it's been brought up by the committee a number of times. My supplementary is around what plans you have in place to making sure that we can retain soldiers, sailors, aviators, and trying to insulate them from getting overseas to Australia, given the Australian Defense Force has got superior pay and better conditions of service.

Yeah. Thank you very much. I think, again, if I may say, a really astute question. We're very mindful of the need to recruit well and it might be that the committee would wish to hear from the Chief of Defense Force and/or chief people officer in terms of personnel numbers coming in.

I won't steal their thunder except to say only that there is a good story to be told there. In terms of then retaining personnel, including with the potential attractiveness of other options within the New Zealand workforce or indeed overseas, I think there are a number of different ways that we need to approach that question. One is that we need to make it worth a young Kiwi, or indeed a mid-career worker who might wish to see a life in service as having great benefits for them at a later stage of life. To think about the reasons that they join, and

to make sure that it's worthwhile, but also that they will be properly resourced and equipped to do the things that they consider worthwhile. And part of that does go to deployments and other opportunities, recognizing they put their hand up to serve specifically for that reason. In terms of looking after them well, again, between us already, we've touched on some themes around housing. But I'd also say the balance of the estate in terms of training and even operational facilities need to be invested in.

There is a considerable work program along those lines. For example, in relation to Devonport Naval Base. And clearly, we need to be mindful of the need to offer opportunities too, in terms of people being well skilled and equipped for doing the role that they can within the New Zealand Defense Force. Of course, I don't want to undermine the essential point from a defense point of view that we need to have smart, talented, hardworking individuals, and we do, operating in teams, of course. But I also think that the Defense Force,

when it plays a role for the benefit of New Zealand Inc. of providing values and ethics and work ethic and particular skills that are valuable to us as a whole, then when they leave the service and go to do other things, on New Zealand soil, we hope, that's a net benefit to New Zealand as well.

A quick supplementary, Tim Costley. Yeah. Encouraging, Minister, listening to some of these questions. I agree with your point it's not just money. They want an adventure, right? We've got to give it to them. But are you open to sort of cross-party support to get all parties coming out and saying, "Yes, we want to see more funding for defense. We support that significant injection, that 2%.

We support deployments." Is that- Yeah. I think that's a really good question. I will say that I've been very pleased with the nature of the interaction and also the tone of the interaction with opposition parties. Opposition with a capital O, only in a technical sense within the parliamentary context, of course.

I think there is a broad agreement across the political aisle at the moment, reflecting the social license that's now present within the New Zealand public for properly equipping our Defense Force to do things that are important in the increasingly uncertain and dangerous world in which we live. I would say, of course, that the point that underlies Mr. Costley's question is a very important one.

I think it would be helpful if we continue to see that degree of support for our Defense Force, recognizing, of course, not only that there is value in a deterrent effect being available when, of course, we ourselves, and in conjunction with allies, partners, and friends, demonstrate that we're able to defend our ideals and interests.

But also, of course, recognizing all the other ways in which the Defense Force helps to keep New Zealand and our cousins in the Pacific, and others around the world, safe. An example, of course, would be search and rescue operations. I think sometimes we make the mistake of considering these to be business as usual, but of course for the- Souls who are saved by maritime patrol aircraft identifying, for example, those who are stricken at sea, and save lives, sometimes of New Zealanders, sometimes of other nationalities.

These are hugely important roles that our defence force can play. So the ongoing support for defence to play those roles, I think would be very welcome indeed. I think our men and women in uniform particularly always notice, and are appreciative when support is forthcoming, in a non-political way, but of course, from the political level of government decision-making.

At this point, I wonder actually, Mr. Chair, if you'll allow me to pass to the Chief of Defence Force actually to provide a real life worked example of very recent past along exactly those lines. Please. Thank you. Just summarizing several of the minister's questions about people, I would assure the committee that the attrition of several years ago, largely driven by COVID, through the COVID period, has stabilized.

Between our uniformed and civilian workforce at the moment, we are sitting at just under 8.5%, and it's been like that for the last year. And we think that that's a reasonably healthy level of turnover for an organization or a workforce, a defence force.

The fact that the attrition has slowed doesn't mean it won't stay low forever. There are periodic and cyclic draws and demands which will see that fluctuate. The problem we're left with, though, is that that high attrition left some major gaps throughout our sailors, soldiers, and aviators, right from the junior levels of people that are fully trained and competent to do their jobs, right up to our most senior people. The challenge for us now is to fill those gaps and you can't just obviously go out and recruit a ship's captain

or a P-8 pilot or a head of engineering in the machine shop, and expect them to be able to deploy a few minutes later. They need to be grown from the ground up. So we're putting quite a lot of effort into recruiting the right people as fast as we can within the parameters that are set for us, which includes budget, but also on retention. Quite a few of these measures that appear in the Budget '26 are going into focusing on retention.

But it's a very delicate balance, and it needs careful prioritization. Every dollar we get needs to be split five ways between people, between equipment, infrastructure that's aged, failing, or we need new types of infrastructure, and operating itself, as well as software and systems like that.

So it's a very difficult equation to balance, and there is never enough resource to go in. We can't just put all our money into people is what I'm saying. We still need to be able to operate. And then finally, it's not just pay that retains people, although it's very important.

As Mr. Kosti said, it's the conditions of service, it's deployments, it's sense of purpose and job satisfaction. There's the people factors around teamwork and morale and leadership. So there's a lot that goes into retention other than just pay in the pocket, although that is fundamentally important.

Right. Was your question in relation to the overview or Yeah, it was sort of a follow-up on one of the comments made. Okay. So, Minister, as we're aware of the comments made by the Americans around pushing us up to 3.5% spend as well, I think the term they used is freeloader. And so I'm wondering what you've got in place to insulate us from those types of expectations, from allies, partners, friends, in terms of the cost of living crisis that we're in.

As you know, where every dollar we spend someplace means there's less of a dollar to spend on our crumbling health structure, support needed in schools, so on and so forth. So I wonder if you've got any plan on how to, first of all, manage that situation with them. But secondly, if you've got any plans that you can share with the committee.

Yeah . Thank you very much. Clearly, there's a lot in that question. Perhaps I can start by acknowledging the fundamental point that social license is important, and we've touched on that already, in terms of the understanding of the New Zealand public about the world in which we're living and the importance of playing our part, including through defense.

Clearly, there is an opportunity cost for every dollar that's spent on defense as compared with health or education services, as you've alluded to. Obviously, I wouldn't agree with your characterization of our health system, but that's another story for another day and another select committee. For the purpose of making sure we invest in defense, clearly the operational outputs are the key driver, but actually, I think it is possible to say that we are able to achieve more than one aim at once, to the extent that we

see additional defense investment as an opportunity for New Zealand defense industry. I think the economic growth potential, including through skills, employment, including in our regions- Is considerable. And if we take a thoughtful approach to the additional expenditure, then it will be possible to realize benefits for the New Zealand public that go beyond the straightforward, but obviously significant outcomes that can be achieved under what you would traditionally think of as a defense heading. Certainly through the defense industry strategy, we

outlined our intention to do exactly that, and there's detail that we can get into, if you should wish, about encouraging New Zealand industry in itself, both to provide sovereign and homegrown ways of providing to our defense force. Of course, if we can encourage them and assist them to export to like-minded nations, I hasten to add, to provide their services and suppliers elsewhere, that's a good thing as well.

And of course, again, those general economic benefits that accrue when we have New Zealand taxpayer dollars supporting New Zealand industry. Where dual use is concerned, as well as strictly military applications, of course, in technological terms, advances in uncrewed technology can be helpful, for example, for agriculture or other ways of Kiwis making a dollar and providing goods and services to their fellow Kiwis.

And that's not inconsistent with the surveillance or search that might take place from the same unmanned aerial vehicles, for example. So, that's one category. I think the social license is important. At a broad level, of course, in terms of New Zealand's independent foreign policy, that will be a subject that will be more helpfully explored with the Minister of Foreign Affairs, whom I understand you'll be meeting with later.

But I think that's worthwhile just to place in context that any discussions with the United States of America or any other sovereign nation, of course, are subject to that general understanding and agreement that we have as New Zealanders, that we have an independent foreign policy. We are a sovereign nation. We do make our own decisions as to the way that we deploy our defense force, but of course, also the decisions we make ourselves with that.

In terms of the reflections about appropriate levels of defense expenditure, as I say, the New Zealand Government, on behalf of the New Zealand people, makes its own decisions in that regard. We have come to the conclusion as a government, and as I say, grateful for the fact that it appears to be broadly supported, that 2% of our GDP expenditure is the right level for us to attain by the year 2032/33, and that's why we're getting on and doing exactly that.

I would actually just say, in relation to the comments reported, made by Secretary of War Pete Hegseth at the same dialogue, defense dialogue that I attended recently, that my response, very shortly following the remarks that he made, was along the lines that we have the best people, and I hear that consistently, and others have heard that too, in terms of our Navy, Army, and Air Force.

We are present everywhere. And yes, we know that our platforms do need upgrading and indeed replacing in some cases. But again, that's a decision we've already made in terms that we are comfortable with. Right. I guess probably my concern there is if they continue to push, and we concede to them, then they'll ask for something else, and they'll ask for something else. So, if you could give the committee comfort that whenever these things arise, with the Americans in particular, that is the position that you'll be sharing with them.

Yeah. I'm happy to restate the comments that I have made since that Shangri-La Dialogue, and even as recently as a couple of minutes ago. Happy to state very clearly for the record that the position of this government is that we will make decisions that we consider to be the right ones for the New Zealand people and that our ideals and interests, consistent with our independent foreign policy in terms of the deployment of any such assets.

And that the comments or the views of other nations are interesting so far as they go, but they're not determinative of the positions and the decisions that we make. Okay. I've got a quick supplementary from Damien O'Connor- Yeah, please... and then we'll jump into the more substantive discussion on DC. Thank you. Well, it's really on the one of expenditure.

Have you set a guideline on procurement so that New Zealand industry and New Zealand manufacturing and other parts of our economy will have access to some opportunities? And if so, what is the KPI that you're going to set? Yeah. There's much that we can say and have been doing along those lines.

To avoid hearing my voice for two hours straight, perhaps at this point I might look in the direction of Secretary of Defense for any comments he might want to make. Thank you, Minister. Yes, is the short answer. For example, prime suppliers, so you're kind of talking about Lockheed Martin or the kind of big multinationals now have to develop a national capability plan that says what economic return will come to New Zealand from a purchase. It's assessed against other bids.

It has a 10% weighting minimum. So, if you're taking a whole lot of factors, price and schedule and competence, there's a significant weighting around what are they going to deliver to New Zealand and New Zealand industry. That's the first point. The second is a whole lot of kind of related, if you think about an ecosystem, so a technology accelerator to try and kickstart New Zealand firms into that higher tech area. The ability for New Zealand firms themselves to be what are called thin primes, which is to act as a

prime, to act as the major contractor, and then subcontract to Lockheed Martin, not the other way around. That's another step we're taking to try and reinforce New Zealand industry. So, there's a range of- Of interventions which are now in place to ensure, if I come back to your opening question around bipartisanship and social licence, New Zealanders have to know that a proportion of that defence spend is going back into the pockets of New Zealanders, and we're very alert to that and acting accordingly.

Thank you. Just to reiterate, as we build the case or the acquisition program for a new capability that we need, compatibility and interoperability with Australia is one of the first questions we ask. But also right up there in those first questions is, is this capability available in New Zealand already, or can we help New Zealand companies develop and field this capability for us? And we're really behind that.

If I could just add, sorry, to that answer. One of the things that we have tended to do in defence over decades is to say we want to buy a ship, so we look for a ship. And sometimes it does have to be a ship or a maritime helicopter. But increasingly, we're wanting to go to New Zealand industry and industry more generally to say, "This is the effect we want.

We want to be able to deliver humanitarian assistance and disaster relief across a coral atoll. You tell us how that might best be done." That gives New Zealand industry a better chance to play in a space that they are better at, which is not building frigates, but coming up with innovative ideas to deliver effect.

So we're trying to have open-ended questions at the start so that New Zealand industry can say, "Well, hang on. It doesn't just have to be the big players here. There's a chance for us to come up with something novel, and let's pitch it to the government." Very good. On that, I think that's a good opportunity to talk more about the opportunity of uncrewed systems and drone-type technologies, obviously, in regards to your comments there, SecDef, around not specifying the physical capability, but more the outcome.

What aspect or what role do you see these systems playing and New Zealand industry within there? We've got some great new companies doing some incredible work already domestically here, and obviously that's a part of the DCP as well. Yeah. It's a great opportunity to give effect to that broad structure of decision-making that the Secretary of Defence has quite rightly outlined, because it's now the case, particularly through uncrewed technologies, that we have the ability to answer questions about the kind of effect we want to provide

in a way that reflects the availability of platforms that aren't necessarily large, so-called exquisite platforms that can be all things to all people. And so I think across the different domains, in air, on the surface of the sea, the subsurface, and of course, land-based drones, which is to say some kind of uncrewed, unmanned, autonomous, or remotely controlled systems.

We have those in New Zealand already producing that. And in addition to saying that this technology exists in New Zealand, is used in a dual-use fashion already for other purposes. Also, it's been employed elsewhere in the defense sense by New Zealand companies, for example, in defense of Ukraine against Russia's illegal invasion of that nation.

I think it's worth encouraging the continued development through the mechanism of the Defense Technology Accelerator that SecDef has referred to. But of course, by engaging closely with understanding and ultimately where appropriate contracting with such business entities. The Defense Capability Plan, as I sense we're sort of moving towards that particular section of the hearing, does include provision for assistant surveillance, as it's known, and including maritime, well, air surveillance, excuse me, of

regions very far south of New Zealand to be polar capable in terms of that capability. Often, of course, we think about New Zealand as a series of islands on a map. Beyond that, we have, of course, our territorial sea at 12 nautical miles, 200 nautical mile exclusive economic zone, the massive search and rescue area out to the Pacific.

But we need to be mindful too, and continue to be present in the Antarctic to protect, of course, New Zealand's scientific endeavors in that part of the world. So if the committee's minded to hear more about what kind of capability could be provided with those uncrewed technologies, then Chief of Defense Force or his delegates would be more knowledgeable, of course, than me. But I'll leave that to you, Mr.

Chair-... as to whether you want to call him now. Yeah, please. I'd point immediately to the great success we've had with our initial foray into uncrewed systems, commercially known as the Bluebottle, but our Navy has shared acquisition with other agencies in New Zealand of two uncrewed systems that have been out and about New Zealand waters and the waters of our friends in the Pacific and the waters in between.

Just to give you an example of that, the two vessels we've named Tahia and Rua, have spent 221 days- At sea on behalf of defence, customs, and police. They have processed hundreds of contacts of vessels of interest in the time that we have had them.

That includes up in Fiji, with maritime domain awareness there of vessels that are moving, either for good reason or suspiciously in and out of the EZs of Fiji. Used extensively, in particular up in Northland for the seasonal yacht arrival period, and also in the Bay of Plenty respectively.

So they started as a trial, and they've been incredibly successful. Some of the funding increase that came out of budget '26 will go into acquiring more of those vessels, similar vessels there. But also, our next immediate project is to develop an uncrewed system that can operate in the Southern Ocean and the waters down there that are notoriously hard to monitor on the surface.

Further down track, we have, as the minister referred to, bigger and bolder uncrewed systems that can fly for longer, sail for longer, cover more area with more sensors, and we will continue to develop those. As a maritime nation, there's an obvious role for uncrewed systems either on the surface, above the surface, or under the surface.

And we've got, over the next few years, a lot of plans underway to develop those. I would just say also, though, that uncrewed systems or drones are not the panacea. They have a lot of limitations, so you need to be careful where you use them.

You use them where safety is a factor. You use them where long endurance or persistence is a factor. And there are certain performance advantages as well that would come from drones. But there is a tendency, from what I can see, to immediately go for what is the uncrewed solution here, because a lot of the time it won't be cheaper and it won't involve less people, because there's always a lot of people back onshore operating, maintaining these things.

The other thing to keep in mind with drones that are operating far from New Zealand is you need the coverage up above in terms of satellites to be able to communicate with, get the mission data, and give the directions to the uncrewed systems. They're great for coastal waters, for instance, but we've still got work to do there as well in terms of satellite coverage and communicating with these things long range.

And that takes more funding and projects to do. Supplementary, Teanaru Turima. Yeah. Actually got kind of questions that probably might be answered by two of yous. One of them was around a conversation that we had at annual reviews around there being a kind of a fund, because you're procuring untested technology.

I mean, drones, five years ago, you could put one up and then you'd go, "Really expensive, mate. Hook them up to JB Hi-Fi at cheaper cost." So if you could clarify for us in what kind of measures that you've got in place to ensure that you're getting the best bang for buck, in terms of that approach, reflecting back to what we've said during annual reviews.

And my other question is around that relationship in the Pacific. As you know, there are a lot of issues in the Pacific. There's now narco submarines cruising around in the Pacific. Some of our Pacific neighbors don't have the same capability of them, and so I'm interested in terms of the relationships there in terms of dealing with issues around IUU, illegal and unreported fishing, et cetera. So how those relationships might be tracking in terms of drone technology.

Yeah. Thank you. Perhaps I can have the first bite of the cherry, and others may wish to add. In terms of that second piece, I think, the potential for surveillance to assist with illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing activity is a subject dear to the hearts of Pacific Island nations.

In terms of the overall context of our relationship with those in the South Pacific, again, Mr. Peters would be more broadly knowledgeable about those. I think we've got the Chief of Army here as well, who can probably add a bit more specific detail to that if you- Sure... would want.
I see a seat by chance. There's a seat- Chief of Army

Thank you. What I would also add to that, it allows for certification and training, and it's also connected with our partners. So you can synthetically connect with the task that you've got given and trained, review what you've done, address where there are areas that you can improve on, and the like. Can you just give us one practical example of a mission, a current mission or a likely mission, and what it actually looks like for your sailor or your soldier that's going?

Yeah. So, let's say we're deploying a combat team overseas. As part of their pre-deployment training, they have a work-up. You can use it both in the work-up to that and practicing, giving them a scenario that they practice. You can also do that internationally. So for example, if you're working up to exercise Talisman Sabre, which we do with the Australians and other partners, you can link into that beforehand synthetically, whilst you also have people in the field conducting it as well. So it connects all of the people

through the operational effects they need to deliver, and you can assess that, work out where the areas are that you need to work on or conversely, if they're certified and meet the requirements. Can I just-- final one, Mr. Chief, if I can. Just, I think for, I don't know how many people are watching this, but just for them, there's a lot of sort of technical and trade terms in there.

But I remember seeing one in the UK where they called it judgmental training, but they would literally come and fly in, and something would pop up, and could you shoot it or could you not? Is it physically like that, or is it more about how do we achieve the mission as a team, and what are you empowered, when are you empowered to make decisions, when are you not?

Can you step us through what it looks like in practice? I think you find it's a combination of both, and it will depend on what you're trying to test at that point in time. Right. Yeah. And this does take the-- we've taken the approach the Australians are using. So this is almost a lift and deliver the exactly- Okay... same capability. Thank you. Greg O'Connor.

Yes. So very well, your defense readiness, the loss of the Manawanui, how has that impacted not only obviously you've a gap to fill in- Yeah... but the lessons learned from that too? My understanding is that Manawanui was not certified for the work it was doing.

Has that meant that there's now, as a result of that finding, you've had to reevaluate a lot of the other work you're doing? Certainly, defense in general, but Navy in particular, is keen to understand the lessons that we need to learn, most obviously to avoid the possibility, even the possibility of such a repeat in the future.

I do need to be circumspect in terms of any comments I make about the processes that have followed, including, for reasons of the court martial process that's now ongoing, and I know you're not inviting me to speak in a way that's constitutionally inappropriate. Well, possibly the certification issue wouldn't necessarily be relevant to the report.

Yeah. I think, I would say there are a number of different factors that coincide, so I'm probably err on the side of safety, so to speak, in that regard. But if there's anything that the CDF or others might wish to speak in that regard, I suppose now would be- So the interim court of inquiry was released the following month after we lost Manawanui.

At that point, and before that, as lessons became obvious around the causes of the loss of Manawanui, they were being implemented from that moment on. We didn't wait till the end of the final report to start that. We started that work as soon as we had an understanding of what went wrong.

The Chief of Navy has put a significant amount of effort into what we call seaworthiness and making sure that the lessons that affected Manawanui have been scoured across the rest of the Navy and our operations to make sure that none of those factors will occur again or none of those factors are present in the rest of the fleet. Just the first part of your question was around the capability.

So, yes, the loss of Manawanui did have some impact on the number of ships we could get to sea at any one time, and we have worked hard to bring HMNZS Otago out of its, what we call care and custody. We had not enough sailors to keep that ship up and running, so we put it effectively into floating storage.

We've been working hard over the last year to bring that ship up to readiness to get it out, and it is nearly ready to get to sea again. And the first places it will go is into the Pacific and around the Pacific Islands to monitor all kinds of fisheries and support the Pacific Islands. And was the Manawanui certified for the work it was doing?

It was certified for a lot of things, but there were some parts where some crew members hadn't been signed off completely for- For what they were doing. That did factor into the court of inquiry, but the leading cause of the loss of that ship was around human factors on the ship on the day.

I'll be absolutely clear on that. Oh. Honorable David McLennan. Just as a supplementary, and I go through the readiness levels provided here by the different three forces. The naval capability, given that the information you've just given us, is very, very high, except in the area of naval patrol capabilities for domestic regional resource and border protection operations.

Arguably, what many people would consider as the most acute and ongoing area- Mm-hmm... where the levels of readiness are very, very low. Just over half, over 57%. The risk being right up there. So I'm just trying to invest- to tease out the accuracy of these figures for you, given that you indicated yourself there wasn't enough staff for the Otago, I think it was.

Yes. So, crew and... But all these other areas would say we're all up and running and ready. Is this perhaps a little misleading? No. Firstly, those figures are audited and scrutinized to the nth degree, so we stand by those figures. As a result of the high attrition, we had to tie up several ships a few years ago. So as we build up our recruitment, work on our retention, and build our numbers of sailors, we can start to bring some of those capabilities back online.

You will see those figures around maritime patrol improve over the coming year when HMNZS Otago comes back online. So it was just low because of that? Well, Manawanui wasn't a dedicated maritime patrol vessel. It was a hydrography and survey ship. But like any of our capabilities, it has a lot of uses. So, it can be used for presence operations or patrolling if needed.

It can be used for diving. It could be used for hydrography. It could be used for a range of things, just like Canterbury can. We can conduct multiple... Our diving capability, we can move between ships. So it is sometimes quite difficult to measure the figures.

It's like a Hercules. It's got specific transport roles, but it does search and rescue as well, and we don't build those into those figures. I appreciate that, but, I mean, the select committee, in its obligation to scrutinize, has to rely on the figures. So what you're implying is that Manawanui's almost just under half of the capability around naval patrol, domestic regional resource, and border protection.

Because we had at this committee, I think it was just one another one, we've got an outbreak of methamphetamine use in this country, and we're not quite sure how it's getting in, but it is. This is part of the border protection system. Yep. And if it's down just over a half or just over 50%, then we're just trying to tease that out, how that is occurring in which you're not the sole responsible agency for that border protection, but you certainly keep part of it.

Sorry. So your specific question around maritime- You're saying because the Manawanui was out, it was a multiple use vessel- Yep... including maritime patrol here, and we're down to 57% readiness. Yep. Mm-hmm. And you kind of implied, well, that was because we've lost it. When Otago comes back on, we'll be up at 100%, which is where everything else is.

We are measured at the end of the year on those, but when Otago comes back online, we will certainly have more capability. If you're concerned about support to drugs flooding our borders and coming in, I can tell you now that we have directed considerable effort of defense people resources to support policing customs around Op Kiwa, and I'd be very happy to give you a briefing or the facts and figures on just how much we're doing in that space.

And very briefly. Thank you, Mr. Chair. If you look at all the other areas, it looks as though except for projection of sustainable capabilities prepared for literal warfare support, which is down at 85%, all other areas of naval capability and readiness are at 100%.

So, implying almost we don't need to put more resources in to what is a fully capable system. That would probably not be right. No. I would not draw that conclusion, no. Okay. Supplementary from Teanu Tuariki. I think mine was a primary, so.

Oh, right. Yeah. Okay. Still on this area? Yeah. Okay. We're just running tight on time, members, so go fire away. Yeah. Mine was around the Army's bicultural plan. I wanted to know where that's at. I've just got a newsroom article in front of me and sort of just noting the- Yes... minister himself, Gosh, getting advice from his own brother, and just to reassure the minister he's probably not the only one to get advice both wanted and unwanted from ... one's relatives.

But I wanted to know where that's at. I know for many of our personnel who are Māori, this forms an important part of who we are. I've gone to major tangihanga, and we see folks get up and do the haka, and it's a beautiful thing. Yeah. So I wanted to know where you're at with that and how you're juggling through that.

Yeah. Thanks very much for the question. Clearly, it's hugely important, the relationship between New Zealand Army and defence forces more generally. But actually, a very particular special relationship with New Zealand Army and the Māori population of New Zealand. There's clearly a very important historical, storied, and hugely valuable contribution made by the 28th Battalion, the Second World War, known of course as the Māori Battalion.

I think it might be helpful if the Chief of Army were to speak to some of that special relationship, including the iwi status. But, in terms of the document that you've referred to, clearly there's always a range of advice that one receives, both official and unofficial. But I'm very satisfied that the appropriate considerations are being taken into account.

It seems to me important that we have a modern, capable army that prioritizes all the kinds of considerations in terms of the way it respects and treats its personnel. And including amongst that is the fact that there are ways that the indigenous warrior culture of New Zealand can be respected, included, and highlighted. I don't think that's inappropriate at all, and I'd certainly support a comment you've made about haka at tangihanga.

And actually, as recently as about a week ago, I had the privilege of attending the graduation ceremony for new officers and sailors of the Royal New Zealand Navy at Devonport. And in addition to the parade ground drill, the reviewing of the parade, all the traditional elements that one would expect in a military graduation or passing out ceremony, there were a number of different cultural elements, of which one was a highly impressive haka that was performed by all of those new officers and sailors. And frankly, it was

hugely impressive, and it adds to rather than detracts from our defense forces, to be able to incorporate cultural elements in a way that does not detract from or is not inappropriately distracting from, of course, the aims that we all share across those services, and will continue to prioritize for the benefit of our nation.

I will just pause there, if I may, Mr. Chair, in case the Chief of Army was minded to say anything further in that regard. But if not, or if in the interest of time you prefer me- Yeah, we might keep going. Thank you. In terms of the announcement earlier this week around the extension of some deployments, in the Middle East and Asia, can you just give us some context around the importance of that for us contributing to those despite our small scale? But not only those, also to other international efforts.

Yeah. Thanks very much, and thanks for noticing and highlighting for the benefit of the committee and those who are taking note, the important contribution that has been made and is now continuing with the extension of those deployments. Very specific details, Chief of Defence Force would be better to describe those than me. But certainly, I'd be remiss if I didn't take the opportunity to thank those men and women who are away from home during those important operations, and of course, the family and friends who support them in New

Zealand. I think the importance of the deployments is of course, in those particular parts of the world. We are contributing to regional security elsewhere, and of course, global security overall. But I'd also make the point that for our Defence Force to be exposed to a range of different environments, to be interoperable and working alongside like-minded nations in those different environments is actually beneficial for us as a Defence Force in terms of our own operational readiness for other tasking as well.

Thank you. We might just leave it at that if we can. Oh, sorry. And we'll jump to the estate piece. Conscious of time, and I want to get to veterans by 11:45. Estate? Yes. Oh, yeah, it can be a primary. So acknowledging improvement of the estate right across the different places as well. And you would've realized that in previous sessions there have been concerns expressed about that, making sure people have got nice warm houses. I was wondering about the situation in Waiouru.

Yeah. About where that's at. When's that coming? Yeah, in terms of housing? Yeah. Yeah. Thank you. Really good point. provide in a suitably long-term way, housing that's suitable for our people, but also is beneficial in other ways. So this year?

to something that might pop up in the near future in this world of disruption? So that might be estate, but it could be one other factors- Yeah... all of the above. I'd say there are always a number and a range of factors that contribute to operational readiness. Clearly, the estates, not only in terms of living conditions, but of course, training, and exercise facilities among them.

There was a useful discussion before around command and other facilities that prepare personnel for operations, and there's nothing particular I'd add to that at this point. Our ability to attract and retain is obviously those people, and there's a lot of training required to get this age person, I guess, up to full capability. So it's not a new issue, I appreciate that. But the efforts and the investment into which particular areas are going to lift that readiness back up to where it should be?

Yeah. Can I simply say that there is no defense base or facility around the country that is in a perfect state. Indeed, a number of them are in a state that might be described very fairly as imperfect. Much work is needed. We are getting on with it, but we do not say for a moment that we have completed that task.

It will be ongoing, of course, by its nature in the way that facilities always need to be maintained well and upgraded and ultimately replaced. We've started behind the eight ball. From my perspective, coming into government two and a half years ago and with previous generations of decision-makers having deferred investment, it's clear there's much more that we still need to do.

But I'm proud at the same time of the progress we have made in the last two and a half years. All right. Any further questions on estate or pretending to be related to estate? It was a good- It was. You did well. Yeah. Okay. All right, we'll move to the veteran section. First question, Greg O'Connor. Thank you, Minister.

Just broadly around veterans, and my interactions with current and ex-service members, effective transition. So we're trying to attract people in, and often they'll spend some fairly good working years of their lives leading up to getting to that 40 year where it's actually quite hard to transition back into civilian life.

Not everyone can end up in Parliament. Some would say that's an ineffective transition, some might have argued. Police would have the same age and perhaps same thing. But just is there work going on in that area? Because that would obviously cross the recruiting, particularly now the need for better technical skills for people involved. Any comments on that?

Yeah. Thank very much for the question, and to the extent that there might be parallels with other uniformed services, just acknowledge your own history in that regard. And reasonable minds may differ on whether we've gone up or down in the world following our time in those respective services. Ask me next year, Minister. Touché. Well, I became a lawyer and then a politician, so I don't know what comes next logically in sequence, but- Councilman... again, others may decide.

I think the very serious- Thing improved... issue that you raise is around the way that we treat people well when they walk through the main gate of the base for the first time and then following a period of service, however long or short. Of course, we need to consider that for that person and his or her family and friends, of course, there's a continuum.

And so we need not consider that person as having ceased to exist from the point of view of them having a service number and so on. But I think it's fair to say while there has historically been a major gap in that regard, the gap is closing. I know that's work that the defense force takes really seriously. Anecdotally, but also in terms of official reports, I've seen considerable efforts being made along those lines.

And I would say that if I were to pass through to the point at which a sailor, soldier, or aviator is no longer in uniform, to consider the contemporary veteran, and to understand that he or she may not be as old or have a particular set of experiences as those we might have considered to be veterans growing up, of a World War II generation, in the case of my grandparents, for example, is actually part of the way that we can make sure that people feel proud of the service that they've undertaken and know that New Zealand is proud and grateful

to them for it. And I think that part of the transition is along those intangible lines, as well as the tangible support and benefit that we accord to people when we allow them to explore opportunities and support them into those following their time and service. Well, just to stop there, because about, I think, 12 to 14% of those with qualifying operation experience are now registered with Veterans' Affairs.

Does that coincide with your own figures? It's a fairly low rate, and obviously, as I understand, there's work now- Going inter-agency work to ensure we pick up those- Yeah ... individuals that might come into contact with other agencies- Yeah ... who happen to be veterans. Yeah.

Is that work that you're aware of and is it- Yeah, very much so. And depending on how you feel about it from a time perspective, Mr. Chair, it might be that we have the Head of Veterans' Affairs join us. Yeah. But certainly, can I say, I know there's a number of different initiatives. Interestingly, two of the government agencies that need to talk well with each other in this regard are the New Zealand Defence Force and Veterans' Affairs.

And historically, it's been the case that they have had to operate in silos by reason of the Privacy Act precluding the easy passage of information from one to the other. And I'm pleased to say, before my time as minister, so I don't want to claim credit for the work of others, but the information-sharing agreement that exists now across different government agencies so that veterans and ex-service personnel have the benefit of a joined-up, coherent, whole-of-government approach is now in place.

And yes, we've got work to do to get up to closer to 100% of those on our books who wish to remain on our books. But there have been positive moves in that regard. And I will say that Mr. Alex Brunt and his team at Veterans' Affairs have been very proactive about getting around the country, physically getting around the country, making themselves available in communities, not only in cities, but remote areas too, to say, "Here is what is available to you.

Here are support mechanisms through the New Zealand Government," but also in terms of non-government organizations that can usefully fill gaps for those who don't have that particular status as well as veterans under the Veterans' Support Act, I should add. So, Mr. Chair, between you and Mr. O'Connor, don't know if you were- Sure.

Well, because I suppose that that inter-agency approach, Mr. Brunt, and you're welcome, is that I note that the funding has reduced for that inter-agency approach in the budget this year for the advice. Do you- Kia ora, committee. Good morning.

Alex Brunt, head of Veterans' Affairs, for the record. I think just to summarize a couple of points. While the uptake of the veteran support scheme sits at around about 13% of veterans, we have around 20,000 to 22,000 veterans registered with us.

So of an estimated population of around about 45,000, that's over 50%. So that's the first thing. As the minister's described, we put a lot of effort into getting around the country through forums and outreach opportunities. That's also had a lot of effort being put in place over the last year around the seamless transition of information and data flow from NZD.

So when a service person gets qualifying operational service now, Veterans' Affairs are automatically notified. We register that person. They will get a pin and a certificate, and they are then in our registration system. So that data flow now happens seamlessly.

In terms of incorporating NGOs into the picture, a lot more work has been done, so the fund has actually increased for next year. There's a contestable grant of 150,000, which will enable us to collaborate more with NGOs. But in terms of actually getting better system interoperability, our focus is on a ICT upgrade, which doesn't normally get people excited.

But you will have heard at the committee before that largely we're in a pen and paper era, and our current system doesn't connect up. So we are replacing that system with a new system that will go live in two years' time, and that will give us the ability for NGOs to dock directly into it. Someone is sitting with a veteran anywhere around the country, they will be able to fill in that form, and it will come directly into VA.

So could someone being who? Well, absolutely anyone. So iwi sit down with veterans, the Returned Services' Association sit down with veterans. But essentially, the forms will be online. You won't have to download something in paper. You'll be able to sit with a veteran online, fill it in, and then that information will come directly into Veterans' Affairs, and it will be able to be triaged that day.

So that will be a vast quantum leap forward for us. And it's part not just of being able to collaborate more with NGOs, but it's about being able to be more responsive and agile with potentially if we have more sizable requirements. Minister, is it the case that a potential reduction in funding doesn't necessarily mean a reduction in output, and that big dollars is not necessarily a signal of success, but rather the delivery of services and potential for efficiency gains? Yeah, I think that's absolutely right, Mr. Chair.

I think importantly, placing the money in the right place. We could have more resource opening envelopes containing paper forms and being entered in manually, in a way that might represent some kind of gains. But I think the kind of technological transformation that Mr. Brunt's described is actually much more helpful.

But I would also make the other point that the cooperation across different government agencies and with the NGO sector being crucial as well, again, isn't necessarily represented in budget allocations. But through the Te Arataki Symposium, for example, that really heavily focuses on the mental health needs of contemporary and older veterans, provides a huge amount of value to the veterans community in a way that goes beyond the way that we might measure, obviously, in a budget, in dollar terms.

Yeah, but given the state of defense- Sorry, next question, Tim Costley. Oh, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, CDF, I think possibly for you, but I wanted to raise a question that a number of people have raised with, uh, me. And so I thought this is an opportune time to, to, to pass it on, which relates to not just veterans, but how we recognize the service of past and present service persons, specifically around Royal honors. And if we look at the last few, um, lists that have come out both at New Year's and at King's Birthday,

I think King's Birthday had two, New Year had one. Um, is, is, is it getting harder to get Defence Force service people and veterans through that process, or are we just not nominating them? We are nominating. Um, we are putting a lot of effort into the way we write and, um, and vet and consider and filter the various awards that come through for various different levels. Um, last week, for instance, um, I, uh, presided over a ceremony which gave out, um, 11 CDF

commendations, uh, and a, uh, Defense Meritorious Service Medal. Um, the Vice Chief of Defense Force behind me chairs the, um, Honors and Awards Advisory Committee two, sometimes three times a year. Um, this is more than we have seen, uh, in the past. Um, the, the, the numbers around, um, defense decorations, DSDs or MNZMs has stayed pretty static over the last, uh, few years.

It would always be good to have, uh, more of those get through and considered by the honors, uh, by, by the, um, by APH. Um, I think just one more point for consideration is that from COVID onwards, um, we did see a reduction in the number of our, uh, deployments, um, number of people able to deploy or new deployments.

Um, uh, that has picked up since COVID again, and we're-- the government has just renewed a number of mandates for ongoing ones. Sometimes the, the apps for which our service people, uh, would receive a commendation or an award, um, take a while to, to get through the system.

Um, so there is a bit of time lag there, but, um, I think if I was to show you the, the numbers of CDF commendations, service chief commendations, other commendations, Defense Meritorious Service Medals, DSDs and Royal Honors, I think you'll find it's been pretty consistent over the last six years.

Yeah, I think the, the, the argument they make and, and they would point back to changes even back in the '90s and, you know, where it used to be early 2000s, uh, significantly more people on the actual honors list. And, um, I, I think I'm trying to characterize their words, but the argument is that the, some of the defense internal ones that, with no disrespect, CDF commendations don't carry the same weight as as, a- a- as the, you know, even a level five or six honor- Mm... um, in the old system. So is it-- I know, I, I think their argument would be, you know, you look at people that

perform really well in their sporting environments. You look at people that perform well or have just served their community on various clubs and groups and, and deserve their King's Service Medal or whatever, or their MNZM, whatever they re- received. But there are a number of people, and you've spoken yourself about the talented people you have and you want to retain and recognize, um, that serve within defense, within the, you know, 10, 12,000 service persons that you have, that, that don't seem to proportionately get that same recognition.

So is there a way that we can... Or is this the function of what we have? Some might say they need to up their level of political donations to political parties. Oh, yeah. That's what they want. I don't think anyone would say that. No? No, I don't think there's ever been a- Yes, there is... case. So, um, yes, I would agree with your observation. If you compared it to, say, 20 or 30 years ago, the number of Royal Honors or less higher-level honors is, um, is lower today.

Um, yes, I would like to see, uh, more of our DSDs getting up and through the, um, appropriate committees for recognition, and more people actually being recognized with DSDs. Right. Greg has one. Yeah. I, so I can't help but wondering some of the issues are a little bit deeper than that with veterans, um, rather than medallic recognition.

Um, because one of the biggest issues, of course, has been that following the, um, Maura Gardner case, um, and the findings by... that, that really we need to be, um, more generous probably, um, and in our interpretation, um, of what veterans require and what treatment should be required.

Have the, the funding and the, the, the, the sort of focus, um, extended? I mean, given- Yeah... you know, there's quite big implications of that case. Are you satisfied that we're going to have the ability to really fully- Um, well, I suppose work on the implications of that case.

Yeah, thank you for the question. Yes, of course, the importance of the litigation relating to the estate of Tawera Gardiner, um, was significant. The government has been responding in a way that is consistent with the ruling of the court. Notwithstanding that prior to that, there had been a different understanding of what Parliament had intended in passing the Veteran Support Act 2014. Additional money has been provided in the budget by way of allowing increased capacity to process the additional claims that have been made.

Of course, within a period of even 12 months or so since that decision had been made, it's been necessary to try to understand the effect of the ruling in terms of the number of people who previously had claims that were not accepted that now, as a result of the ruling, would be. And so, that's a complex area, and we're seeing that play out in real time. But I do want to take the opportunity to commend Veterans' Affairs for responding very effectively to that.

And of course, we'll provide the necessary resource to meet our legal and moral obligations to our veterans, regardless of what interpretation of that legislation may be preferred. And will veterans be required to reapply, or will those previous applications be sufficient to be revisited?

Where a veteran has had a decision made on their claim, they would need to reapply to the department. All right. Te Ururoa. Yeah, just sort of some of the constituents and veterans who have been in contact with me as well. So, I've got a couple of quick questions, which you might have quick answers for.

One of them is actually in an email that's sitting on your desk- Ah, okay... so I might be a little bit more fuller there. One of them was sort of following up from what Greg was saying around the Veterans' Support Act. Are you looking at reviewing it? Is it fit for purpose given what happened with the Tawera Gardiner case?

So, some quick comments there as well. And another one was around whether Veteran Affairs will adopt statements of principle used in Australia for traumatic brain injuries- Ah... and injuries caused by toxic gases from the soldiers. Yeah. I have two questions that- Yeah. Thank you for the questions. I think I can answer them quite quickly. Mr.

Chair, and I'm conscious of your timing. On the second one first, I don't want to be specific about the scientific or medical basis for particular statements of principle, but I will say that those do reflect, um, and they are updated from time to time in a way that reflects the shared understanding between New Zealand and Australia. In terms of the prospect that we might review the Veterans' Support Act, post Tawera Gardiner's litigation, but also more generally, I think it's always the case that ministers and agencies should think about ensuring

that their legislation is appropriate and fit for purpose. So, there's nothing to preclude us continuing to consider that matter. Whether or not a formal review would be necessary at this time, I think I would acknowledge that there have been broad discussions about recognition and support to veterans raised recently, um, in the Patterson Review at one point, not that long ago.

But also in terms of broader discussions about whether a covenant relationship exists between the people and government of New Zealand on the one hand and ex-service personnel on the other. And we've had some things to say about that, and I'm committed to ensuring that recognition as well as support, with a capital S, per the act, is provided to our ex-service personnel.

All right. Thank you very much. Members, that brings us to the end of the session. Minister, thank you very much for your time today and your comprehensive answers. We really appreciate that.
